claim: Three types of agents (local, background, cloud) serve different needs: local for hands-on iteration, background for 50/50 control, cloud for full delegation
actionable: Use local agents for critical tasks (tests) with human in loop; background agents for big tasks with git worktrees; cloud agents for documentation/non-critical work
source_title: Cooking with Agents in VS Code — Liam Hampton, Microsoft

claim: Git worktrees enable background agents to work on isolated branches without blocking main development; safe sandbox for autonomous work
actionable: Spin up git worktrees for background agent tasks; isolate work to subdirectories; let agents push PRs from worktrees; approve before merging to main
source_title: Cooking with Agents in VS Code — Liam Hampton, Microsoft

claim: Cloud agents in GitHub Actions are safe: isolated environment, white-listed network access, no direct main branch push capability, MCP server access for testing
actionable: Use GitHub Actions for cloud agent tasks; restrict network to whitelisted domains; grant MCP servers (Playwright, GitHub) for testing; require approval before merge
source_title: Cooking with Agents in VS Code — Liam Hampton, Microsoft

claim: VS Code unifies local, background, and cloud agents in one control plane; Copilot CLI, Claude, and third-party agents accessible from single interface
actionable: Configure VS Code extensions for all agent types; use settings modal to manage custom agents, skills, hooks, MCP servers; centralize agent orchestration
source_title: Cooking with Agents in VS Code — Liam Hampton, Microsoft

claim: Custom agents with specific knowledge (e.g., test-writing agent familiar with codebase patterns) outperform generic agents on domain-specific tasks
actionable: Build custom agent personas with detailed system prompts; include codebase patterns, style guides, testing patterns; expose domain-specific tools
source_title: Cooking with Agents in VS Code — Liam Hampton, Microsoft

claim: Using Autopilot mode allows background agents to make tool calls without asking for confirmation; reduces friction but requires careful task design
actionable: Enable Autopilot for well-scoped background tasks; disable for high-risk operations; always monitor background agent progress and output
source_title: Cooking with Agents in VS Code — Liam Hampton, Microsoft

claim: MCP servers extend agent capabilities; Awesome Copilot is open-source and supports migration to non-Microsoft tools via MCP abstraction
actionable: Explore Awesome Copilot project; set up MCP servers for external tools (Azure, GCP, documentation); enable cross-platform agent flexibility
source_title: Cooking with Agents in VS Code — Liam Hampton, Microsoft

claim: Running one codebase with three parallel agents (local testing, background UI building, cloud documentation) on same tasks demonstrates multiplexing benefit
actionable: Design systems that support multiple agents on different aspects of same project; run in parallel; merge results; minimize cognitive load on developers
source_title: Cooking with Agents in VS Code — Liam Hampton, Microsoft
